data.title: Silver And Gold: The Rise And Fall Set Up An Option Play
data.description:
Silver and gold volatility reached extreme levels

My goals for this article are twofold. I want to A) explain why investors should be prepared for persisting high volatilities for both silver and gold, and B) explain why I see a favorable setup to sell options under current market conditions. In recent trading data, I have witnessed some of the most extreme volatilities in silver and gold prices in my experience (and I have been investing in both metals for more than a decade by now). As an example, the next chart illustrates the percentage decrease from recent price highs for the iShares Silver Trust (SLV) and SPDR Gold Shares (GLD) over the past month. You can see how both metals, after staging some of the most spectacular bull runs in the past year, suffered their sharp downturns. Specifically, SLV plummeted almost 33% off its high, and GLD dropped more than 12% off its high.

In tandem with the price volatility, the implied volatility (IV) for both metals has already reached extreme levels in the option market. For instance, the screenshot below was taken from MarketChameleon and provides the IV of SLV. As seen, SLV's IV is currently 110, approximately tripling its 52-week historical average of 34 and only about 5 points shy of its 52-week peak of 115. In terms of percentile, SLV's current IV puts it in the 99th percentile rank. In other words, the current level of IV was only observed 1% of the time in the past year.

Against this background, I will next explain that such heightened volatilities could persist for both metals.

Be prepared for persisting high volatilities

Looking ahead, I anticipate such elevated volatility to persist for both gold and silver due to several reasons. My anticipation is largely based on considerations that fall into three buckets: lack of technical guidance, ongoing geopolitical risks, and the conflicting forces for and against a potential short squeeze.

Let me start with the first one. Despite the sharp corrections, the prices of both metals are still well above their multi-year (or even multi-decade) resistance levels. As such, I expect both swing traders and long-term investors to keep dealing with the lack of technical guidance to anchor their future trades and target prices.

As for the geopolitical risks, a key catalyst behind the recent rise and fall of precious metal prices is the uncertainties surrounding the Federal Reserve's next leadership, which in turn leads to the uncertainties regarding the Fed's future pivots. Even though President Trump has just confirmed his nomination of Kevin Warsh as the next Fed chair (which the market interpreted as a net negative for gold and silver), I won't automatically assume that this nomination has completely ended the uncertainties on this front. The nomination still needs to be confirmed, and other ramifications could develop in the meantime.

Finally, there is also a potential for a short squeeze, especially for silver, given the violent price swings and also the inventory decrease lately. As a reflection of this potential, the next chart shows COMEX's silver inventory in the past 1 year. As seen, since Sept 2025, the inventory has experienced a sharp decline from around 550 million oz to the current level of 408 million only. The ongoing price correction certainly helps to counterbalance the odds of a short squeeze. In the meantime, other counterforces have also been set in motion, with regulatory intervention among the top forces on my list (see a recent announcement made by the Shanghai Futures Exchange below). I don't expect such a confluence of conflicting forces to find their equilibrium point smoothly and/or quickly and expect continued volatility in the process.

CaiXing Global News: The Shanghai Futures Exchange on Monday tightened trading rules for key metals for the second time in a week, seeking to tamp down excessive market volatility after a dramatic surge in silver and gold prices. Starting after Wednesday's close, the exchange will expand daily price limits for copper and aluminum futures to 9% and raise margin requirements for speculative positions to 11%. Similar adjustments will be applied to international copper contracts. In addition, daily opening position limits for non-futures-company members and clients trading silver and tin will be reduced starting from Monday night's trading session.

Gold and silver: options

Given such extreme volatility, I see a good setup for investors to sell options. For investors unfamiliar with options, when IV rises, the option sellers receive a higher premium income from the option buyers. Probably it is easier to explain the dynamics with a specific example. The table below shows the put option chain for the SLV ETF with an expiry of Feb 27, 2026, which is less than 1 month away from today. For an NTM (near-the-money) option with a strike price of $75, the option last traded at about $10.93 per share. In other words, the seller of this option gets paid almost 15% of the share price upfront in exchange for the obligation to buy SLV shares by or on Feb 27 at a price of $75. If the option is not executed, the seller thus effectively earned a 15% yield in less than 1 month (assuming the option is covered by cash). In the case the option is assigned, the seller would have effectively bought SLV shares at about $64 per share only, a quite attractive entry point for long-term holding if you share my following outlook.

But as a strong word of caution, the risk calculus for trading options can be very different from direct share ownership. I don't want my above discussion to mislead you to believe that there are no risks for option sellers either when the option expires or gets assigned. When viewed from the perspective of opportunity cost, the risks for option sellers can be unlimited.

Other risks and final thoughts

My above discussion on the put option is equally applicable to investors interested in call options. I used the example of a put option largely due to my personal view on the long-term prospects of silver. As you could have already guessed, my analysis with the option play is targeting the immediate term (1 or a few months). For the longer term, I have a quite positive view toward silver and gold. I see our transition towards green energies and a digital future as an unstoppable trend and expect this trend to keep expanding the industrial demand for silver. For gold, I expect continued demand from central banks as they diversify their currency reserves. These reasons are further detailed in my other articles (for example, a recent one explores the similarities and differences between today's situation and the so-called Silver Thursday in 1980). Given such a long-term view, I prefer the use of a put option as a potential way to accumulate more silver shares.

Before closing, let's also take a quick look at GLD's IV and options. The screenshot below is also taken from MarketChameleon. It shows the current IV of GLD, and the picture is just as extreme as that for SLV. As seen, GLD's IV is currently 42.5, more than double its 52-week average of 18.5. In terms of percentile, GLD's current IV is also in the 99th percentile rank, just like SLV. As such, most of my comments regarding SLV's options in the foregoing section are perfectly extendable to GLD options in my mind. Again, I personally would prefer the use of put options to accumulate more shares at a lower cost basis for long-term holding.

To conclude, the thesis of this article is mainly twofold. Firstly, I wanted to caution investors about the factors that could keep volatilities heightened for silver and gold. Secondly and more importantly, I wanted to explain why I see a favorable setup for option sellers under this outlook. Investors who want to wind down their precious metal exposure should take a look at call options, and investors who want to maintain (or increase exposure like me) should consider put options.

data.title: FDMO: Momentum Investing Can Beat The S&P 500 In 2026
data.description:
After significantly outperforming the S&P 500 in the past three years, the Fidelity Momentum Factor ETF (FDMO) is likely to continue its surge in 2026 despite concerns over geopolitical tensions. The large and mid caps robust earnings growth power, interest rate cuts, and economic growth support the bullish trend. The valuation-related risk also declined due to a drop in forward valuation from the recent peak. Therefore, I initiate coverage of FDMO with a buy rating.

Momentum Investing Could Earn Market-Beating Returns in 2026

Momentum investing is one of the best strategies for aggressive risk-tolerance investors to generate lofty returns in the short to long run. This strategy works well mainly in bullish market conditions. A risk of sharp selloff is also high during price corrections and bear trends. In the past three years, the Fidelity Momentum Factor ETF significantly outshone the broader market index, delivering a price return of 100% compared to the S&P 500’s 73%.

In 2026, I expect the continuation of the uptrend due to multiple reasons, including rate cuts and economic growth. The Fed rate is currently hovering in a range of 3.50% to 3.75%, compared to the recent peak of 5.50% to 5.75%. The market expects two more rate cuts in 2026. On the other hand, the US GDP surged at a whopping 4.3% year over year in the September quarter. The Fed Atlanta anticipates more than a 5% increase in the final quarter of 2025.

Whereas, corporations have also been boosting investor confidence through their robust earnings growth power. The S&P 500 is expected to report 15% earnings growth in fiscal 2026 compared to a 12.5% projected increase in 2025.

AI, which played a key role in the recent uptrend, is expected to fuel tech companies earnings growth to 28% in 2026. The sector is expected to report a 25% earnings increase in 2025. Furthermore, rest of the S&P 500 sectors are also likely to produce solid earnings growth, with expectations for double-digit growth from the materials, industrials, financials, health care, and utilities sectors.

Overall, the macro market fundamentals and corporate performance support the stock market bull run. Data also indicates that war can create only a temporary decline in markets, with an average selloff of 5%. Other than volatility in oil prices, the potential war between the United States and Iran is unlikely to create a significant impact on stock markets. Iran has limited business relations with the global community.

FDMO Uses an Attractive Momentum Strategy to Beat the Market

Given a price return of 100% in the past three years, the Fidelity Momentum Factor ETF appears to be a worthwhile investment vehicle. FDMO tracks the performance of the Fidelity Momentum Factor Index. The index seeks to identify stocks with strong price momentum to generate market-beating returns.

Its stock selection strategy is based on a composite score of various factors, including total returns, positive earnings surprises, volatility-adjusted returns, and low average short interest. It ranks stocks through a rules-based, sector-neutral methodology. The portfolio rebalance occurs on a monthly basis.

With this strategy, FDMO’s portfolio is spread across all the sectors, with a higher weight in the fast-growing technology sector. In addition, the communication and consumer cyclical stocks collectively make up 20% of its portfolio weight. The fund’s portfolio also offers exposure to the high-momentum stocks from the financial, healthcare, and industrial sectors. The other S&P sectors make up a small weight in its portfolio.

FDMO’s portfolio comprises 130 stocks, with the top 10 accounting for 38% of its weight. The fund’s strategy of diversifying its portfolio across all sectors with a limited weight in any holding helps in lowering a risk factor while capitalizing on the broader market trends.

FDMO’s top 10 holdings include all the Magnificent 7 members. These mega caps have been playing a significant role in the bullish price movement. Each year over the past three years, their contribution to the S&P 500 price growth was more than 50%. FactSet data indicates that the group is expected to generate 22% earnings growth in 2026. In addition, most of these companies are leading in the AI market.

FDMO’s other top holdings include Micron Technology (MU), Lam Research (LRCX), JPMorgan (JPM), Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A) (BRK.B), Palantir Technologies (PLTR), Amphenol Corp. (APH), Seagate Technology (STX), Visa (V), Oracle (ORCL), Gilead Sciences (GILD) and Mastercard (MA).

FDMO Characteristics

The Fidelity Momentum Factor ETF trades at a higher valuation compared to the S&P 500 index due to its exposure to the fast-growing companies. FDMO’s trailing twelve-month price-to-earnings ratio is 31, and its price-to-book ratio is 6.46%. This is up from the S&P 500’s trailing PE of 26 and PB of 5.5. Whereas, FDMO’s expense ratio of 0.15% is lower than the median of all ETFs of 0.50%. The fund’s average shares trading volume of 37K is in line with the median of all ETFs.

FDMO’s Competitors

iShares MSCI USA Momentum Factor ETF (MTUM), Invesco S&P 500 Momentum ETF (SPMO), and Vanguard U.S. Momentum Factor ETF (VFMO) are among the popular momentum ETFs.

Over the last twelve months, all these ETFs delivered solid price returns to beat the broader market index. However, FDMO was the largest gainer. VFMO is one of the most diversified funds, with more than 670 stock holdings. Whereas, MTUM and SPMO track the performance of high-momentum stocks from the S&P 500 index. FDMO and MTUM’s expense ratio is 0.15%, while SPMO and VFMO’s expense ratio is 0.13%.

Risk Factors

Momentum investing carries a higher risk factor compared to the broader market index. For example, the momentum ETFs declined at a higher pace during the tariff-related selloff in early 2025. Moreover, FDMO and other momentum ETFs can also respond to tech and AI-driven selloffs because of their greater exposure to the technology sector. The economic and monetary conditions can also impact their performance. FDMO’s standard deviation of 14 and annualized volatility of 22% are higher than the median of all ETFs of 9.6 and 16.5%.

In Conclusion

I believe there are strong signs of the extension of the bull run into 2026. Therefore, momentum investing is likely to perform well. Investment vehicles, such as the Fidelity Momentum Factor ETF, can enable investors to access a diversified portfolio of momentum stocks through a single investment. In recent years, FDMO has highlighted its potential to offer market-beating returns. Its expense ratio is low, and liquidity is in line with the market average.

data.title: Canadian Solar: Growth Under Pressure In A Slowing Market
data.description:
Canadian Solar (CSIQ) is a Canadian company that sells key infrastructure for solar power generation: modules, cells, wafers, and ingots. It uses semiconductor equipment to manufacture its infrastructure, especially silicon wafers. But the most important part of its business is the sale of solar modules.

The projected growth of the company accompanies the advancement of renewable energies. Renewable sources are already part of the energy systems, to a greater or lesser extent, of the major energy producing countries. However, I'm not seeing the trend of accelerating growth in the future very clearly. And this was a central point of my maintain rating for Canadian Solar.

I consider renewable sources to be in a transition phase in 2025, especially with the changes that Donald Trump has implemented and the new energy demands caused by AI data centers. In a lower but no less important order, cryptocurrency mining and electric cars (along with their energy requirements) will contribute to the growth in demand.

The U.S. is cutting almost $30 billion in loans for renewable energies as part of its political change regarding the previous Democratic administration. All clean energy sources are affected, both wind and solar. Trump seems to be more focused against wind energy than solar energy, but it is still targeted. But beyond the political vision of the time, I'm one of those who believe that we'll need energy from all possible sources, both renewable and traditional. I wouldn't rule any of them out.

Understanding clean energy trends

Besides energy trends, I tried to better understand the renewable sector. I wanted to analyze solar energy trends and figure out if it will be an attractive investment for the long term. First, I focused on renewable energies, which, counting all their sources, will represent almost 42-43% in 2030. In this universe, solar energy still has a good growth path, representing 15.7% in 2030 versus 10.2% in 2026.

Although it is still very good long-term growth, I noticed a little slowdown compared to previous years, which may be normal given that a decade ago, solar energy was still in its infancy. The company itself shows that it's projecting a deceleration. In the following image, you'll see that annual installations of photovoltaic solar power have been steadily increasing and will continue to do so. China represents most of those facilities

However, the comparison shows a lower 6-year CAGR rate than the previous period. From 2019, with 118 GW installed, to 2024, with 599 GW installed, I got an approximate CAGR rate of 35%. Post-installation growth remains high but drops sharply to 5%.

Although in the image you can clearly see that China is the country that is demanding the most solar energy, the company's shipments are concentrated in the U.S., with 44% of module shipments, representing 5.1 GW. This is important because although it operates globally and aims to supply Chinese demand, the U.S. remains its primary market. In that line, the company built a plant in Mesquite, Texas, in 2023 and is looking to continue expanding its local production.

Despite the good intentions, I'm seeing a more complex scenario for solar energy in the U.S., both for domestic companies (such as First Solar, FSLR) and for foreign companies, like Canadian ones. Additionally, we must consider the global deceleration.

An opportunity for the company is to bet on the Chinese market, where the demand is stronger, but China has its own local manufacturing. Of the top eight companies that operate in this resource extensively, the first six are Chinese in terms of installed capacity, so if Canadian Solar focuses exclusively on China, it will face fierce competition.

Q3: operational deceleration

In addition to the long-term pressures I'm seeing on solar energy, Canadian added some operational adjustments to its energy guidance, following the presentation of its Q3 2025 results. Now it expects a range of 24.5-24.7 GW of shipments of solar modules, when in the previous quarter they were expecting shipments in the range of 25-27 GW. It is not a serious slip, but it confirms a drop in shipping capacity compared to previous projections as well as compared to 2024, which had shipments of 31.1 GW.

Along with the adjustments, the guidance for battery storage sales was also moderated, and therefore, so was total revenue. Total revenue estimates for 2025 are in a range of C$5.7-C$5.9 billion, whereas before the upper limit was C$6.3 billion. The only surprise was in the gross margin, where the company presented an improvement compared to the previous estimate of 120 basis points.

Total income was C$1.5 billion, a year on year decrease of 1% and a 12% decrease compared to the previous quarter. Gross margin, as I showed you before, I think it was the best number to show, and I saw it influenced the control of total expenditures, which fell 10% YoY. The gross margin went up/down 80 basis points YoY. This is a very good cost control in the context of income drop. Meanwhile, the company reported another net loss after a Q2 with profits of C$45 million. EPS also turned negative, with a loss of C$0.07.

Battery storage solutions: a potential catalyst?

Weak module sales were a central part of explaining the drop in revenue: shipments per GW fell 38% year on year. This also caused the fall in the gross margin, by 33%. But beyond the weaknesses in the modules, Canadian Solar is also betting on battery energy storage solutions and highlighted a record shipment in the quarter of 2.7 GW.

Battery storage revenue isn't broken down as a separate line item in the financial statements, but I understand it's integrated into Canadian Solar's main segment. I did see the numbers of the previous years, and you can see it in the following image.

In 2024, battery storage generated income of C$815 million, and the estimate based on the contracted portfolio is that it will generate revenues of C$3.1 billion between 2025 and 2026. Battery storage does have a future of greater expansion compared to solar energy.

Beyond the fact that they are two shared segments, where one supplies the other, my feeling is that this market has more room for expansion because countries and companies that need energy find it more practical to use GW through batteries. It's a newer business than renewable energy itself, and, in fact, it offers the possibility to expand the consumption of clean energy, which is naturally more intermittent because it depends on specific climates. The expected CAGR rate for this segment globally is 41%, with the years 2021-2031 taken as the floor.

China is, once again, the major expanding market for batteries, but in this case, the same leading players in solar energy are advancing investments in this segment. In other words, Canadian Solar will continue to deal with competitors if it seeks to focus on China. Besides, my feeling is that competence is more difficult due to the debt growth and the leverage of the last quarters. In the following image you can see the growth in assets, but you can also see the weakness in the operational cash flow and in the debt levels.

My optimistic valuation model did not yield an attractive target price

To better analyze the intrinsic value of the company, I focused on, for the moment, setting aside my thesis of the slowdown of solar energy. I focused mostly on the optimal use of the potential energy storage in batteries, which I believe still has a high capital expenditure due to the continuity of the solar infrastructure. To link that optimistic scenario, I have elaborated a DCF model with that perspective. I really liked the contracted battery storage portfolio figure of C$3.1 billion in the short term. That's why the projection of a strong income increase of 30% and 13% in the short term is based on that initial impulse.

The EBIT margin would also initially rise sharply and then moderate to 3.7%, surpassing the current negative margin. You should take into account that there is a very strong capital investment. In the last 12 months, CapEx was C$1.6 billion, while the depreciation and amortization were C$923 million. I have moderated that difference until a positive convergence in my model, assuming that the company will achieve efficiency to consolidate a strong free cash flow. I have used a perpetual growth rate of 3%, in line with the inflation and the global average growth, an 8.5% WACC, and a tax rate of 19.1%.

The result of my model, with a very optimistic impulse based on the growth of the energy storage segment, gave me a very conservative upside. My conclusion is that the current price reflects a premium valuation because it is among the largest solar energy companies globally. Even so, I consider that the sector needs new catalysts to clarify my doubts about the explained deceleration.

Risks

The company has risks related to debt growth, which, if combined in the long term with weakening revenues and negative cash flow, could make operational growth more difficult. What I'm seeing is that all of this is happening in just a part of clean energy, solar energy, which could slow down in the following years, as I tried to explain throughout the article.

Geopolitical factors are also important, both between the U.S. and China (a market of potential expansion for CSIQ despite strong local competition) and between the U.S. and Canada. Trump's latest warning to Canada was to impose 100% tariffs, which would negatively impact sales of solar equipment, representing 44% of CSIQ's portfolio shipments.

Conclusion

Canadian Solar is a key player within the global solar energy market and has a strong installed capacity to supply any increase in demand for solar energy. Battery energy storage is a catalyst that the company is leveraging and will seek to further increase in the future. It is, I believe, the best catalyst of all.

But for the moment, solar modules represent most of its income, and it will continue to grow during 2026, based on slide 14 of the investment presentation. As I see a weaker demand for solar energy and a more complex debt balance with falling income, I consider that the most appropriate rating is Hold.

data.title: ProKidney Stock: Differentiated CKD Approach Positions Rilparencel Favorably
data.description:
ProKidney Corp. (PROK) is technically a kidney disease cell-therapy company. They develop their leading drug candidate called Rilparencel. This asset is an autologous kidney cell therapy for chronic kidney disease (CKD) related to type 2 diabetes (T2D). The drug is in pivotal Phase 3 trials under PROACT 1, which is intended to support a potential accelerated approval submission. After that, PROK promptly plans to do a longer follow-up study and seek full FDA approval. The FDA has confirmed that the estimated glomerular filtration rate (eGFR) slope can serve as a surrogate endpoint to support a Biologics License Application (BLA) under an accelerated approval pathway. So, on balance, I feel it’s still fair to remain bullish on the stock as long as you have a favorable long-term view on Rilparencel.

Promising Accelerated Approval Odds

ProKidney Corp. is a clinical-stage biotech working on an autologous cell therapy to preserve kidney function. This can be life-changing for patients who present advanced chronic kidney disease (CKD), particularly associated with type 2 diabetes (T2D). The company was founded back in 2015 and is currently headquartered in Winston-Salem, North Carolina. I also previously covered PROK in January last year with a “Buy” rating, and since then, the stock has appreciated by roughly 17.9%. So, I thought it was worthwhile updating my analysis on this name.

As a quick recap, PROK’s core candidate is Rilparencel (REACT), which is a patient-derived cell therapy. They essentially take a percutaneous kidney cortex biopsy, then grow and enrich the selected viable renal cells ex vivo. After that, PROK’s approach formulates these cells in a gelatin-based hydrogel and finally injects the cells back into the kidney cortex. This is done during an outpatient procedure guided by a computed tomography scan (CT).

This way, PROK’s therapy aims to slow the decline of kidney function by restoring and supporting kidney tissue health locally. Since the procedure is autologous, the immune rejection risk is reduced. So far, PROK’s Rilparencel has also secured the FDA’s Regenerative Medicine Advanced Therapy (RMAT) designation, which I believe showcases that regulators probably have a positive view on this asset as well.

Having said that, PROK actually positions Rilparencel as a complementary treatment in a late-stage population. They’re targeting its use as a near-term preparation for dialysis and transplant. PROK argues that fewer kidney drugs have been proven in stage 4 CKD patients, and these patients are underrepresented in many of the trials that define the current standard of care. PROK states that it’s not right to assume that stage 2 or 3 patients included in most trials will present results that can be fully translated to stage 4 cases. Therefore, they believe this gives them room for a therapy like Rilparencel that’s specifically designed for advanced CKD. In short, they think Rilparencel could eventually claim a very unique and differentiated role within the stage 4 niche demographic.

Also, PROK argues that there is an unmet need that Rilparencel can fulfill. Basically, they believe that while available therapies for diabetic CKD are helpful, they don’t actually stop the disease’s progression. Therefore, patients still lose kidney function in the long-run. In its latest corporate presentation, PROK shows charts that compare SGLT2 inhibitors (dapagliflozin) versus placebo over time, using the estimated glomerular filtration rate slope (eGFR). Those charts show that both decrease in eGFR, meaning kidney function continues to decline with dapagliflozin and placebo. But, those slow declines don’t stop deterioration, which is why PROK’s SGLT2 inhibitors help as a complement despite not being intended for preserving kidney function in advanced cases.

Rilparencel's Path Ahead

In any event, Rilparencel is now in a Phase 3 trial called PROACT 1. PROK aims to obtain an accelerated pathway approval for the drug, using a surrogate endpoint. Basically, the surrogate endpoint acts as a proxy for actual effectiveness. In this case, PROK’s surrogate is their eGFR data, which shows how fast kidney function declines over time. The FDA has agreed on this eGFR surrogate endpoint, which means PROK can prove Rilparencel’s efficacy for delaying progression toward kidney failure this way.

After that, PROK can use its eGFR data to file a surrogate endpoint Biologics License Application (BLA). And, if all goes according to plan, PROACT 1 can be used for both an accelerated and full approval submission. This is vital from an investment perspective, as PROK believes it can produce its eGFR slope analysis by Q2 2027. Furthermore, PROK says that the accelerated approval slope analysis depends on approximately 360 patients, most of them already recruited for the PROACT 1 trial. In Q2 2027, in the trial cohort, the median tenure of patients will be around 14 months.

PROK believes this projection should be enough to run a statistically powered comparison of the eGFR slope of Rilparencel versus a control arm designed to mimic the experience without delivering the active cell therapy (sham). With that in mind, PROK has also initiated the expansion of its in-house manufacturing capabilities. They acquired two adjacent facilities in Winston-Salem, North Carolina. PROK is using these facilities to produce Rilparencel in its Phase 3 trial and later scale manufacturing to commercial volumes (assuming they reach regulatory approval, of course).

Valuation And Risk Analysis

Now, from a valuation perspective, PROK currently trades at a $640.8 million market cap. Their latest 10-Q from Q3 2025 shows their balance sheet held $95.3 million in cash and $176.4 million in marketable securities. That amounts to approximately $271.7 million in available liquid funds for their R&D and operations, against only $33.3 million in total liabilities and no financial debt. Unfortunately, their book value remains at -$1.0 billion, but that’s mostly due to their accumulated losses of -$1.25 billion. Otherwise, I think they have a very healthy balance sheet.

Nonetheless, according to Seeking Alpha’s dashboard on PROK, the company is not yet projected to generate any meaningful revenues this year. And their 10-Q also states they don’t expect to generate any product sales in the near future. But they do explicitly mention that Rilparencel could lead to collaborations or licensing agreements that may generate some revenues.

So, I think at this point they could quickly unlock substantial shareholder value by simply announcing they’ve partnered with some big pharma company for Rilparencel’s R&D and commercialization. Plus, PROK’s presentation mentions Rilparencel's market opportunity could cover up to 1 million people in the US who suffer from stage 3b/4 CKD and diabetes. In other words, there’s clearly a substantial upside scenario with this asset in the next 2-3 years, in my view.

Naturally, this remains an inherently speculative bull case. After all, I estimate they’re still burning through $31.8 million every quarter (as of Q3 2025). Note that I got that figure by simply adding its quarterly cash flows from operations and CAPEX. That would suggest a cash runway of about 8.5 quarters. However, management’s runway guidance into mid-2027 likely assumes their cash burn will increase in the next quarters as they double down on PROACT 1 and a potential accelerated approval process with Rilparencel. Therefore, I do think we’ll see their cash balances decline much faster in the coming quarters, which could become worrisome for some investors. It also increases their dilution risks, and an exceedingly tight runway can contract their valuation multiples as well.

Lastly, this is a very thinly traded stock with only 1.2 million shares in average volume. At the current $2.00 PPS, that implies a shallow trading liquidity of $2.4 million, so even small buy/sell orders can push the price considerably. And this can be a major issue if they decide to sell shares directly to the market to raise more cash. Besides, positive Phase 3 results aren’t guaranteed either, and by then, their runway will be much tighter, so please remember that PROK remains a highly speculative stock.

Conclusion: Still Bullish Long-Term

Overall, I think PROK’s R&D efforts with Rilparencel continue in the right direction, and the FDA’s openness to an accelerated approval with eGFR is a huge win. This would expedite their approval timelines in the bull case scenario. However, there are still clinical trial risks, because if their earlier results don’t hold up in their Phase 3 testing, then the stock would undoubtedly trade much lower. Also, their runway is clearly going to be a major issue if they don’t secure an accelerated approval for Rilparencel, meaning dilution risks remain in the cards. Yet, on the other hand, I feel the upside scenario based on their credible Phase 2 data and the FDA’s agreement for a surrogate endpoint warrants a more optimistic take on the stock. That’s why, on balance, I still think PROK is a very compelling speculative “buy” for long-term investors. Just make sure you size your position accordingly.

data.title: Best Dividend Aristocrats For February 2026
data.description:
2025 Review

The Dividend Aristocrats finished 2025 on a sour note, on average, declining by 0.44% during the month of December. The ProShares S&P 500 Dividend Aristocrat ETF (NOBL) faired marginally better with a loss of 0.37%. Meanwhile the S&P 500 measured by the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY) rose marginally in December, by 0.08%. The final 2025 return for NOBL clocked in at 6.82%, while SPY delivered a far superior return of 17.70%.

But some individual Dividend Aristocrats faired much better in the prior year. 32 Aristocrats outperformed NOBL and 16 bested SPY.

Best Dividend Aristocrats in 2025

Here are the best-performing Aristocrats in 2025:

  1. Cardinal Health (CAH) +76.26%
  2. Albermale (ALB) +67.70%
  3. Caterpillar (CAT) +60.32%
  4. C.H. Robinson Worldwide (CHRW) +58.98%
  5. Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) +47.49%
  6. Nucor Corporation (NUE) +42.05%
  7. International Business Machines (IBM) +38.27%

data.title: 13-Week Money Supply Growth Reaches 6.5%
data.description:
Money Supply is a very important indicator. It helps show how tight or loose current monetary conditions are regardless of what the Fed is doing with interest rates. Even if the Fed is tight, if Money Supply is increasing, it has an inflationary effect.

One key metric shown below is the “Wenzel” 13-week annualized money supply figure. It was made popular by the late Robert Wenzel, who tracked the metric weekly as an indicator for where the economy might be headed. In 2020, the Fed started reporting the data monthly instead of weekly. It should also be noted that Money Supply data can be heavily revised in future months.

Recent Trends

Seasonally Adjusted Money Supply has been growing on a consistent monthly basis since November 2023 (26 straight months). The latest month (December) showed a modest increase of $89B, the third largest increase since April. The continued growth in money supply is inflation. The rising prices are an aftereffect of this money growth. Fed Chair Powell seems oblivious to this fact, as he said he has no concern over the rapid rise in gold prices.

Figure 1: MoM M2 Change (Seasonally Adjusted)

The increase was 4.9% annualized, which is above the 4.6% over the past year.

Figure 2: M2 Growth Rates

December average is typically around 5.5%, which puts the latest month somewhat below average.

Figure 3: Average Monthly Growth Rates

Non-seasonally adjusted shows positive growth for the months of December and January (note: this data is ahead of the seasonally adjusted data above). December was the strongest month since March.

Figure 4: MoM M2 Change (Non-Seasonally Adjusted)

The weekly data shows 6 consecutive weeks of positive money supply growth, which is the first time that has happened since January of 2025.

Figure 5: WoW M2 Change

The “Wenzel” 13-week Money Supply

The late Robert Wenzel of Economic Policy Journal used a modified calculation to track Money Supply. He used a trailing 13-week average growth rate annualized as defined in his book The Fed Flunks. He specifically used the weekly data that was not seasonally adjusted. His analogy was that in order to know what to wear outside, he wants to know the current weather, not temperatures that have been averaged throughout the year.

The objective of the 13-week average is to smooth some of the choppy data without bringing in too much history that could blind someone from seeing what’s in front of them. The 13-week average growth rate can be seen in the table below.

Growth decelerated for 3 weeks, 2 months ago, but has now been accelerating for 7 straight weeks. Growth has reached 6.5% annualized, which is the highest rate in a year.

Figure 6: WoW Trailing 13-week Average Money Supply Growth

The plot below shows how the latest year compares with previous years. Since we are at the start of a new year, it is hard to see where the trend is going. 2025 stayed right in the middle of the range the entire year. It seems doubtful 2026 will do the same with a new Fed chair set to be appointed.

Figure 7: Yearly 13-week Overlay

Inflation and Money Supply

The chart below shows the history of inflation, Money Supply, and Fed Funds. As shown, in 1970, inflation worked with a ~2-year lag compared to Money Supply. Money Supply slowed dramatically in 2023 and 2024 but has been moving back up. Inflation has also been stickier than the Fed would like, but unfortunately, they cannot do much given the large debt load of the US Government and Corporations. Despite inflation staying elevated, even moving up in the recent period, the Fed has no choice but to continue lowering rates.

The inflation rate is now only 1% below the fed funds rate. That is not a big enough gap to slow inflation, especially when the money supply is still expanding. Expect prices to continue rising in the years ahead and the Fed to only fan the flames.

Figure 8: YoY M2 Change with CPI and Fed Funds

Historical Perspective

The charts below are designed to put the current trends into historical perspective. The orange bars represent annualized percentage change rather than raw dollar amount.

Figure 9: M2 with Growth Rate

Below shows the 13-week annualized average over history. This chart overlays the log return of the S&P. Mr. Wenzel proposed that large drops in Money Supply could be a sign of stock market pullbacks. His theory, derived from Murray Rothbard, states that when the market experiences a shrinking growth rate of Money Supply (or even negative), it can create liquidity issues in the stock market, leading to a sell-off.

While not a perfect predictive tool, many of the dips in Money Supply precede market dips. Specifically, the major dips in 2002 and 2008 from +10% down to 0%. 2022 was highly correlated with a fall in Money Supply and the rebound has corresponded with the big stock market move we saw in 2023 and into 2024. The slowing growth in 2025 did correlate with the market drop in March/April, but that was clearly more headline-driven. With money growth continuing to climb, it seems to be providing enough fuel to keep pushing the market higher. The November market correction was shallow since the money supply remained elevated

Please note the chart only shows market data through Jan 5th to align with available M2 data.

Figure 10: 13-week M2 Annualized and S&P 500

One other consideration is the reverse repo market at the Fed. This is a tool that allows financial institutions to swap cash for instruments on the Fed balance sheet.

Reverse Repos peaked at $2.55T on Dec 30, 2022. Money gushed out from March 2023 to May 2024. The outflows have yet to return, which is likely driving the continued money supply growth.

Figure 11: Fed Reverse Repurchase Agreements

Wrapping Up

Inflation is truly defined as the expansion of the money supply, which generally leads to higher prices. Gold did not reflect this for over a decade but is now playing catch-up. It is the canary in the coal mine, and it is saying that things have finally gotten out of control. Although this was obvious for years, it is coming to a head rather quickly. The growth in money supply for decades is the support driving gold higher. The metal decided to move all at once instead of doing it in an orderly fashion like the money supply expansion.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Powell Industries: Profitability And Growth Justify The Premium - Still Bullish
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Powell Industries (POWL) was founded in 1947. Through the decades, it has gained a reputation for itself in designing, developing, and manufacturing custom-engineered equipment and systems. Their custom-engineered equipment and systems help large-scale businesses to control, distribute, and monitor the flow of electrical energy. Their end consumers range from oil, gas, and petrochemicals, which require safe and reliable energy to process liquids and gases (such as LNG, refining, petrochemicals, biofuels, etc.), to Industries that require/produce large amounts of electrical energy (such as the electric utility market, transportation, metal and mining, and data centers, etc.).

As the technology advances, the global demand for electrification and the upgrade of existing systems increases, and 2025 saw a robust demand as well. The company is expected to release its quarterly results for 1Q26 soon, and it is expected to beat the earnings estimates as it did in the previous quarters, as projected by the analysts. POWL stock surged by more than 81.38% in the past year. I was bullish on Powell stock in my previous article, and I am still bullish on POWL and rate it a “Strong Buy.”

Fourth Quarter and Full Year 2025 Performance

Powell Industries (POWL) reported revenue of $298 million in 4Q25 compared to $275 million in the same quarter last year, reflecting an increase of 8% YoY. Similarly, full-year revenue increased by 9% to $1.1 billion in FY25 compared to $1 billion in FY24. According to the company, the increase in revenue is attributed to the gain in the Electric Utility and Light Rail Traction Power markets. These markets surged by 100% and 85% in FY25, respectively.

Powell reported a net income of $51 million in 4Q25 reflecting a 12% increase as compared to the net income of $46 million in the same quarter last year. Full-year results show a strong increase in the net income. In FY25, the company reported a net income of $180 million compared to $149.8 million in FY24 increasing 21%.

By looking at the increasing backlogs of the company, I am confident that it gives predictability about its income sources for the next 1-1.5 years. Powell’s FY25 backlogs are $1.3 billion. The company’s new orders seem pretty decent at $1.1 billion in FY25. The company’s book-bill ratio is 1.0x, which shows Powell’s efficiency in converting orders into sales. I am bullish on POWL due to its increasing backlogs, and I think this will push the stock price higher.

Another fascinating thing that I admire about POWL is that it’s a debt-free company. Its balance sheet shows that its cash and cash equivalents are increasing with each year. In September 2020 the company’s cash and cash equivalents were $179 million, and now at the end of FY25 they have a liquidity of $476 million. With the money they have, they can either spend the money to expand their business or deliver value to their shareholders through dividends and stock buybacks. With a 28.2% ROE, increasing cash and cash equivalents, and zero debt, I think POWL is well-positioned to grow further, and that is one of the reasons I like POWL.

In order to deliver value to the shareholders, the company has provided a road map of capital allocation. As of now, the company has a cash balance of $476 million. Out of this $433 million the company intends to spend:

$11 million on R&D

$13 million on CAPEX

$13 million on Dividends (Increasing Trend>> good sign for income-seeking investors)

$13 million on Jacintoport Expansion (I’ll discuss this later in this article)

With the rest of the cash, between $150-175 million, the company has an option to spend it on business expansion.

Project Expansion And New Acquisition

Recently the company announced plans to expand its Jacintoport project and intends to spend around $13 million. With this amount, the total investment in this project will reach $20 million over the past 8 years. Powell’s Chairman and CEO, Brett A. Cope, said that this expansion will help them to support the strong and durable cycle of Oil & Gas order activity over the next 3-5 years.

At the end of the third quarter, Powell announced the acquisition of Remsdaq Ltd. for a total of $16.3 million, and the transaction was completed during the fourth quarter of 2025, all in cash. Remsdaq is a U.K.-based SCADA Remote Terminal Units (RTUs) manufacturer. RTUs help in power generation, transmission, and distribution because of their ability to efficiently automate and manage electricity flow. I think this acquisition will significantly strengthen Powell’s electric automation platform. The combination of Powell’s hardware and detection sensors combined with Remdaq’s SCADA RTUs will offer smarter and more complete solutions. This means that utilities like electric companies will get better and smarter tools to run their systems. This will bring more efficiency, lower costs, smoother operations, reliability (fewer outages and faster fixes), and security, meaning protection from cyber-attacks and failures. This acquisition, I think, will eventually bring a better brand value, and Powell will reap its fruit in the future.

Valuation

For most of the time, Powell was treated as a cyclical industrial company. But for the past few quarters, Powell’s revenues have been consistent, margins are solid, and profitability has improved. Once assigned lower sales and book multiples, and earnings discounted heavily by the market, the market is becoming more optimistic about POWL.

Valuation ratios of POWL show that it is trading at a premium, but that premium is justified with its operating performance, earnings quality, and outlook as shown in the FY25 earnings report. Its recent FY25 results showed robust results, high margins, increased backlogs, and earnings growth, due to which I am bullish on POWL.

Its P/E Non-GAAP TTM of 28.51x and forward P/E Non-GAAP of 27.08x compared to a sector median of 22.4x and 22.42x, respectively, show that the POWL stock is trading at a premium. In my opinion, a company like Powell that has delivered strong earnings in the past quarters, with increasing backlogs and margins, this premium is not excessive. Before, their earnings were inconsistent, and as of now, their earnings are more reliable and repeatable than before and will likely continue to hold. This is due to stronger backlog quality and improved execution by the management. So I think this premium is justified considering its earnings and growth.

Its PEG GAAP TTM ratio is close to 1.36 despite having a higher P/E ratio. This tells us that the earnings growth is keeping pace with valuation. In simple words, I would say that POWL stock is becoming profitable faster than it is becoming expensive.

Powell's EV/EBITDA (TTM) is 20.73 and EV/EBITDA (FWD) is 19.54 with sector medians of 14.81 and 12.94, respectively. This tells us that POWL's premium is backed by stronger profitability, operating leverage, and cash generation. On the contrary, its EV/EBIT ratios are slightly below the sector median. I can conclude that, accounting for depreciation and capital structure, POWL stock's premium is not overstretched. I would say it's a quality premium, not a red flag.

Price/book ratios and price/cash flow ratios further strengthen my bullish case. They are high due to Powell’s ability to generate significantly higher returns on equity, convert earnings into cash consistently, and efficiently utilize cash. Once ROE structurally improves from its current 28.2%, book value becomes irrelevant, and you should focus more on cash generation and reinvestment returns. So if you pay a premium in such a situation, it's more logical than excessive.

What to Expect In Q1 2026?

Powell’s backlogs in FY25 were $1.3 billion, of which 824 million is expected to be recognized in the fiscal year 2026. According to Seeking Alpha, the revenue of POWL in the first quarter of 2026 is expected to be around $255.34 million. This is lower than the previous quarter, but it's due to the fact that demand is lower in the winter. As a result, revenue shrinks in this quarter each year. Its impact on the full-year earnings is negligible as demand peaks during the third and fourth quarters. Powell's CFO, Michael Metcalf, also mentioned that their strong backlogs combined with order activity in FY26 mean they are hoping to close another successful year. I think since they have no liability, strong backlogs, expanding projects, and with the acquisition of Remsdaq, they will be able to consistently increase their revenue. The main catalyst for its increasing revenue is the surge in the demand for Powell's products in the electric utility market.

Powell's EPS estimate for the first quarter of 2026 is 3.14. Last year in the same quarter, the EPS estimate was 2.61 and the actual EPS was 2.86. POWL has been successfully beating the estimates in the previous few quarters, and it is expected to do the same in Q126 and the upcoming quarters as well since they have strong backlog quality. It is almost impossible for a company to increase its earnings without increasing its revenue. As I said, the upcoming quarters will potentially increase its revenue; this will eventually increase its earnings.

Risks

No company is risk-free, and like others, POWL also has risks associated with it. Till now Powell was treated as a cyclical industrial company, but since their revenue has consistently increased for the past few quarters, it is considered to continue the trend. But we just can't ignore the fact that it can't enter that phase again when revenue was inconsistent and not so reliable. It is heavily dependent on the oil and gas sector, as most of its revenue comes from this sector. This makes Powell vulnerable to price swings. Any significant shift in the oil and gas sector, either through government policies, trade wars, or pricing, will have a direct impact on their earnings.

Another risk is the adoption of AI in its technology. If they slowed down on integrating AI into their machines or if some other competitor produced efficient machines using AI, it would pose a significant threat to their business.

Powell is investing in acquisitions, which are so far delivering good results, as seen in the FY25 results. But overinvesting could backfire if demand projections are not met.

Conclusion

In my opinion, POWL stock is well-positioned for entry, and I am expecting the bull run to continue in the near future as well. Its fundamentals are strong, and its business is expanding. Most importantly, the company is generating a billion dollars in revenue per year and is still a debt-free company with increasing cash and cash equivalents. They have plenty of cash ($476 million as of FY25) for acquisitions and project expansions. Also, they have quality backlogs of $1.3 billion, and 60% of it is expected to be recognized in FY26. This makes me bullish on this underrated stock.

That's why I am rating POWL a “Strong Buy.”

data.title: Bonterra Energy: Focusing On Charlie Lake After Strong Recent Results
data.description:
Bonterra Energy (BNEFF)(BNE:CA) announced excellent Charlie Lake results from its most recent two wells, with initial production that was over double its type curve. It also announced a Charlie Lake acquisition that adds around 36% to its Charlie Lake land holdings.

The strong Charlie Lake results and expanded inventory increase my estimate of Bonterra's value from CAD$5.70 to CAD$6.00 per share. This is based on long-term (after 2026) US$70 WTI oil and CAD$2.50 AECO natural gas.

Bonterra is projected to generate US$13 million in 2026 free cash flow, while organically growing production by approximately 3%.

This report uses US dollars as the main currency along with an exchange rate of CAD$1.36 to US$1.00. Thus, my updated estimate of Bonterra's value is around US$4.41 per share.

Charlie Lake Acquisition

Bonterra announced an acquisition of Charlie Lake assets adjacent to its existing operations. It is paying CAD$15.7 million (US$11.5 million) in cash for the acquisition.

Bonterra mentioned that it is adding around 760 BOEPD in production, including 240 barrels per day in light and medium oil production, 40 barrels per day in NGLs production, and 2,885 MCF per day in natural gas production.

Bonterra is also gaining 21 Charlie Lake drilling locations from the deal and intends to drill two gross Charlie Lake locations on the acquired acreage in 2026.

Bonterra is funding the acquisition with credit facility borrowings, and received an increase in credit facility commitments from CAD$125 million (US$92 million) to CAD$150 million (US$110 million). Thus its liquidity is increasing by approximately CAD$9 million (US$7 million) overall.

Charlie Lake Development

Bonterra has recently demonstrated strong Charlie Lake results, contributing to its decision to increase operations there. It completed two gross (1.8 net) Charlie Lake wells in Q4 2025. These three-mile laterals had combined peak 30-day production rates of 2,650 BOEPD, including approximately 1,100 barrels per day of light oil. This appears to be over double the peak 30-day production rate for Bonterra's three-mile Charlie Lake type curve (at 566 BOEPD per well).

Bonterra is putting around 60% of its 2026 capex budget towards Charlie Lake. It is planning on completing 1 gross (0.9 net) DUC well there in 2026 as well as drill and complete 6 gross (5.7 net) Charlie Lake wells.

Preliminary 2026 Outlook

Bonterra expects approximately 16,300 BOEPD in 2026 production, which would be roughly 8% production growth compared to 2025, although this would be reduced to 3% production growth without the acquired Charlie Lake volumes.

Bonterra's preliminary guidance includes expectations for roughly 6,650 barrels per day in light and medium oil production.

The current strip includes roughly US$64 WTI oil and US$1.80 (per mcf) AECO natural gas prices. AECO hasn't benefitted from the recent winter storms, as Western Canadian production was unaffected.

At those commodity prices, Bonterra is projected to generate US$198 million after hedges. Bonterra's 2026 hedges are projected to have US$1 million in positive value, with slightly negative value oil hedges and positive value natural gas hedges.

I am increasing my estimate of Bonterra's value from CAD$5.70 to CAD$6.00 per share. This new estimated value translates to US$4.41 per share at the current exchange rate.

This increase in value is due to Bonterra's recent Charlie Lake success and the inventory it has recently added. The changes in 2026 commodity prices (weaker natural gas prices and stronger oil prices) since I last looked at Bonterra roughly even out in terms of their effect on its projected free cash flow.

I am maintaining my longer-term (after 2026) commodity price expectations at US$70 WTI oil and CAD$2.50 AECO.

Conclusion

Bonterra Energy is focusing on Charlie Lake development, putting 60% of its 2026 capex there and making an acquisition that added 36% to its land holdings. These moves make sense given Bonterra's strong Charlie Lake results to date, including two recent wells that had early production that was more than double Bonterra's type curve.

Although AECO natural gas strip prices have gone down recently, oil prices have improved to US$64, and Bonterra should be able to generate US$13 million in 2026 free cash flow while growing organic production modestly.

I am thus increasing my estimate of Bonterra's value slightly to CAD$6.00 per share.

data.title: High Tide: Why The Market Is Wrong After Q4
data.description:
High Tide Inc. (HITI) reported record fiscal Q4 results for the August-October period. The company’s primary Canadian cannabis retail business continues to thrive, resulting in record revenue and EBITDA for the quarter. Other businesses reported more mixed performances but have positive catalysts ahead – Remexian’s hiccup seems temporary, and the underperforming ecommerce segment has a speculative opportunity ahead in the U.S. market. Concerns have clouded the market’s sentiment on the stock, creating a widening undervaluation.

I maintained a Strong Buy rating in my previous August 2025 article on the stock, titled “High Tide: Good News With German Entry, Strong Q3”. The stock has since lost -24% of its value, losing previous gains. The S&P 500 has returned 9% in the same period.

Q4 Was a Record Quarter for High Tide as Canna Cabana Grows

High Tide’s fiscal Q4 report came with great financials. Total revenues reached C$164.0 million, up by 19% year-on-year and beating the analyst consensus by C$5.0 million. High Tide reported an adjusted EPS of C$0.02 at twice the analyst expectation of a single cent. Adjusted EBITDA reached C$12.4 million, up by 51% year-on-year as High Tide’s thin margin profile is gradually improving.

The performance was again led by Canna Cabana cannabis retail in Canada. The brick-and-mortar segment showed 15% revenue growth to C$150.7 million, reflecting quite a similar pace from previous quarters as Canna Cabana continued to slowly gain market share in its five operating provinces.

Canadian Cabana Club members reached 2.5 million with 45% year-on-year growth, and paying ELITE members reached 151 thousand with a sharp 107% year-on-year increase. The discount club business model’s success is reflected in Canna Cabana’s great 5.5% growth in same-store sales. Notably, the high-margin Cabanalytics data revenue stream also showed 20% year-on-year growth to C$13.1 million.

SNDL Inc. (SNDL) most recently reported noticeably slower 3.6% growth in same-store sales in cannabis retail. The competitor, and minority shareholder in High Tide, owns the Value Buds and Spiritleaf store chains.

The brick-and-mortar segment’s adjusted EBITDA grew by 40% to C$14.1 million, driven by a 339 basis point increase in the gross margin. High Tide has previously been aggressive in its pricing to compete for more market share, but pricing power now seems to be improving as the competitive landscape is easing. Given that High Tide’s margin profile is still thin, such a gross margin gain has a massive impact on earnings.

Reaccelerated store openings did cause pressure on margins as High Tide has to expand operating expenses before sales mature in new locations. Canna Cabana's underlying earnings power should reveal itself as stores mature; the long-term margin outlook is much better than the 9.4% brick-and-mortar EBITDA Q4 margin. In retail, same-store sales growth is a key earnings driver, and Canna Cabana has performed exceptionally well in the metric. Gross margin gains ultimately still more than offset SG&A pressure in Q4.

Momentum in brick-and-mortar sets up High Tide for a successful FY2026. The company targets opening 20-30 new stores during calendar 2026, and is on a path towards 350 stores over the long term. High Tide currently operates 218 stores.

Remexian Was Weak, But Likely Only Temporarily

The Q4 report was High Tide’s first quarter with Remexian’s financials included; the German majority acquisition was completed on the 2nd of September, meaning that around two months of Remexian’s financials are shown in the report.

High Tide’s medical cannabis distribution segment showed quite shy revenue of C$9.8 million and breakeven adjusted EBITDA; Remexian’s financial performance was much slower than when the acquisition was announced. The Q4 report suggests annualized revenue of approximately C$59 million compared to an implied C$113 million annualized level for the October 2024 to March 2025 period. Lower revenue and weak gross margins weakened adjusted EBITDA to a breakeven level from a strong 21% margin.

In my opinion, the hiccup isn’t cause for concern yet; the acquisition's performance will become questionable only if such weakness persists throughout FY2026 as well. High Tide notes Remexian’s slowdown to be due to temporary supply chain disruption in Portugal, leading to lower sales and weaker margins on aging biomass, and expects the headwind to subside by the end of Q2. Supply chain disruption is gradually subsiding, and High Tide is positioning the segment for growth in FY2026 in Germany and the U.K. among other European markets. The wholesale business can also have quite high revenue variance on a quarterly basis depending on shipment schedules.

December was the business’s second highest month by volume, hinting at a recovery after Q4. High Tide even recorded a C$20.9 million increase in the company's derivative liability value, reflecting higher multi-year EBITDA expectations than previously that lead to a higher expected buying price for the remaining 49% of Remexian.

Watching Cabana Club’s International Launch: From Divestment Discussions to Speculative Potential

High Tide’s ecommerce business has trailed expectations; Cabana Club’s international ecommerce launch hasn’t been very successful. The segment generated just C$3.5 million in revenue in Q4, more than halving from a year ago. Low pricing led the segment’s gross margin to decline to just 25% from 57%. Aggressive operating cost cuts did improve the segment’s adjusted EBITDA slightly to a -C$1.7 million loss, improving by around C$0.1 million from a year ago despite dramatically lower gross profit. The segment remains a clear drag on High Tide’s total earnings.

To address the ecommerce segment’s performance, High Tide has already looked at strategic options such as divestments, licensing partnerships, and maintaining paired down websites until U.S. cannabis reform happens. High Tide notes that it has received an offer for its ecommerce platforms.

Divestment discussions have since been put on pause in December, though. Trump signed an executive order on the 18th of December, rescheduling marihuana from a Schedule I drug to Schedule III and leading to an announced CBD pilot. In the pilot, expected to launch in early 2026 already, senior Medicare beneficiaries could receive up to $500 in annual hemp-derived CBD products when recommended by a physician. High Tide’s U.S. CBD brands, NuLeaf Naturals and FAB CBD, could be positioned to benefit significantly from the pilot program.

Ultimately, I still believe that High Tide’s ecommerce segment holds minimal value. The international Cabana Club launch hasn’t yet attracted a significant number of members, questioning its value in establishing the Canna Cabana brand in international markets. The segment continues to pressure earnings. The U.S.’s CBD pilot could represent a meaningful opportunity for High Tide, but the opportunity remains highly speculative for now.

HITI Stock Is Now Massively Undervalued

I revised my discounted cash flow (DCF) model. I again assume High Tide to ultimately acquire the remaining 49% of Remexian with 6.1 million shares, C$12.8 million in debt, and $C12.8 million in cash. I also add 10% dilution to outstanding shares to account for stock-based compensation.

I have kept revenue expectations the same from FY2026 forward; Remexian’s 10-month addition will boost growth greatly for FY2026, but otherwise, momentum is primarily driven by Canna Cabana’s success in Canada in my DCF model. I don’t factor in significant organic growth for Remexian for now due to market volatility. The FY2025-FY2035 CAGR is set at 7.6% with 2.5% perpetual growth afterwards.

Canna Cabana’s margin expansion, Remexian’s typically higher margin profile, and moderating losses in ecommerce should improve High Tide’s margin profile over the long term. I expect the EBITDA margin to reach 10% in the next few years, below the company’s 12% long-term target to be conservative.

Lease expenses, new store investments, working capital investments, and taxes weigh on High Tide’s cash flow conversion from EBITDA. I expect free cash flow conversion to be relatively weak in the next couple of years as High Tide’s growth investments remain high, but to gradually improve to around half of EBITDA.

The estimates put High Tide’s fair value at $4.67, suggesting massive 103% upside as of writing. The stock’s price action has remained sluggish, not reflecting High Tide’s fundamental performance as Canna Cabana continues to shine. Some caution regarding Remexian and international ecommerce is warranted, but concerns are clearly over-emphasized in the valuation.

The fair value estimate is down from $5.28 previously.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of capital [WACC] of 9.34%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I again estimate a very high 12% interest rate, but quite a modest 10% long-term debt-to-equity ratio. For cost of equity, I use Canada’s 10-year bond yield of 3.42% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 4.18% is Professor Aswath Damodaran’s most recent January estimate for the US. I have kept the beta estimate at 0.94. With an ESG addon of 1.5% and a liquidity premium of 0.5%, the cost of equity stands at 9.40% and the WACC at 9.34%.

Risks

The bullish thesis doesn't come without significant risks. The two side businesses, Remexian and ecommerce platforms, represent the most probable ones.

A changing regulative landscape in Germany and other European markets, and disruption in Portugal, could continue to weigh on Remexian. High Tide has significant ambition for the wholesale business, which could fall short if regulatory or other risks arise. As is always the case with acquisitions, integration comes with risks.

The ecommerce segment's losses are another key risk. If High Tide doesn't materialize any potential in U.S. CBD, the business will likely continue to generate losses for the company. Discussions regarding potential divestments could falter after High Tide has put them on hold; the received offer for the ecommerce business doesn't fully mitigate the segment's risk.

Takeaway

High Tide reported record earnings in Q4, led by Canna Cabana’s continued momentum in Canada. The retail chain’s expansion and great store performance are clearly showing, creating great long-term earnings potential as pricing power improves and newer stores’ sales mature. Concerns regarding Remexian’s temporary disruption and the ecommerce segment’s underperformance weigh too much on the market’s sentiment; HITI stock is massively undervalued according to my DCF valuation. I continue to hold my position in HITI and remain at a Strong Buy rating.

data.title: Global Trade In 2026: Significant Slowdown Amid Large Shifts
data.description:
By Julian Geib, Junior Economist, Global Trade | Rico Luman, Senior Sector Economist, Transport and Logistics

Global trade has proved resilient, but will take a hit in 2026

2025 will go down in history as the year everything changed in global trade.

When US President Donald Trump announced a sweeping set of tariffs in April using the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA), global trade was jolted into sudden adjustment. Expected price increases sparked a widespread wave of frontloading, especially in the US, even bringing forward the container shipping peak season as importers rushed to secure goods for the second half of the year.

Despite these disruptions, global trade proved more resilient than anticipated. The US, the world’s largest economy, represents roughly 14% of global imports, and many affected Chinese exporters successfully diverted shipments to new markets.

Overall, we forecast global trade volume to end FY2025 at 4.2% year-over-year growth, surpassing expectations. Most of this growth stems from strong numbers in the first and second quarters and can largely be attributed to frontloading effects. In the third and fourth quarters, trade growth slowed substantially, due to correction effects, and we expect a further flattening.

For FY2026, we therefore believe that, because frontloading effects will disappear, global trade growth will remain limited to just 0.5-1%.

Global trade is not diminishing, but slowing and changing

Index volume (2020 = 100)

Tumultuous start of 2026 sets the tone for lasting volatility and lower growth

The start of 2026 underscored that volatility is here to stay. The US president escalated his efforts to take control of Greenland and threatened European countries with additional tariffs. The same applies to Canada, which is seeking to deepen trade relations with overseas partners, including China.

These episodes illustrate a broader pattern: the US could continue to view tariff measures as a tool of strategic policy. And more events lie ahead, such as the trade deal with China expiring in October 2026. The crackdown on free trade means momentum is expected to weaken further in 2026. Higher barriers will continue to force trade adjustments and dampen global trade growth, despite some bright spots such as intra-Asia trade.

Trade adapts, but future growth will be more tempered

Global imbalances seem to have reached their limits. Specialisation and globalisation have created significant economic benefits – especially in the 1990s – but they have also created dependencies that are now being scrutinised. And this comes at a cost. Global merchant trade growth has lagged GDP growth for several years, and rising protectionism is further weighing on its potential.

As geopolitical tensions intensify and efficiency is a subordinated priority, trade has entered a new period of slower growth and greater fragmentation.

The days of strong supply chain globalisation are over

Global merchant trade volume and global GDP %, per period

Significant regional differences beneath the surface

While global trade exceeded expectations, regional dynamics reveal significant divergences. Asia remains the growth engine of global trade and is likely to continue outpacing the world. In contrast, eurozone external trade has barely expanded in recent quarters. Elevated energy costs in Europe continue to weigh on industrial exports, particularly to China and the US.

Germany – the largest economy in the eurozone – experienced substantial shifts in trade flows in 2025. Chinese exporters increasingly stepped in as a major competitor in typical German strongholds such as cars, pharma and industrial components. As a result, German exports outside the EU slowed across the board, with the most pronounced weakness in shipments to China. Nonetheless, due to the higher import volumes, China surpassed the US as Germany’s largest trading partner in 2025.

US policies mark a paradigm shift and the start of a new era

The current US tariff framework has triggered a global recalibration and the start of a new era. The administration has pledged to reduce the budget deficit and redistribute part of tariff revenues to households, and it is likely to maintain this approach. Although a Supreme Court ruling may strip Trump of his tariff-setting authority under the IEEPA, he can still invoke other legal tools embedded in various statutes.

In addition to renewed discussions about the expiring US-China trade deal, the key trade agreement between the US, Mexico and Canada (USMCA) enters a review period this year. Official statements have already hinted at the possibility of the deal lapsing altogether – or being replaced with separate bilateral agreements.

Besides US politics, global uncertainty continues to stem from multiple conflicts. The situation in Ukraine and the Middle East, alongside broader concerns about geopolitical stability, peace and democracy, weighs heavily on confidence. As a result, households and businesses remain cautious, holding back spending and investment.

Efforts to reduce dependency on China, but the US and Europe cannot easily shift away

Despite declining exports to the US, China's trade surplus reached a record $1.2 trillion in 2025. China continues to advance its export-driven economic model and has successfully expanded shipments to other regions, including Europe, Latin America and especially other Asian economies. Many of these Asian countries are increasingly acting as re-export hubs and extended manufacturing bases, with Vietnam being a prime example.

China has effectively preserved its role as the world’s factory. At the same time, countries such as Vietnam, South Korea, and others are expanding their manufacturing capacity as companies implement ‘China plus one (or two)’ strategies. These countries are taking on a larger role in the assembly and re-export of Chinese parts as well. This is one of the key drivers behind the rapid expansion of intra-Asia trade.

In 2026, we will likely see more pronounced policy efforts in both the US and Europe aimed at reducing dependency on China. In the EU, the Critical Raw Materials Act and potential ‘Made in Europe’ requirements are notable initiatives. However, reducing reliance is easier said than done. China's production base is often simply too large to substitute for local resources and supplies in the short term. Moreover, shifting production comes with a substantial cost. At the same time, further pushing export growth to Europe against lower prices is expected to see more (political) backlash. In practice, most relocations are occurring within Asia – a pattern of regionalisation – rather than being reshored to Europe or other high‑cost regions.

On the European side, the new Mercosur deal could offer some upside potential for trade flows with countries like Brazil and Argentina when provisionally imposed. However, most of that impact will probably materialise later than 2026 as tariffs will be phased out gradually. For now, the symbolic value of strategic independence and the signalling effect outweigh trade benefits.

But the ongoing discussion and concern within the European Parliament are also a signal that we’re living in different times. At the same time, the urgency to diversify and bolster other relations is there. The adoption of further agreements – such as the recently concluded EU‑India deal – offers new long-term opportunities and could help to diversify supply channels. In the meantime, boosting trade within the internal market may offer the largest opportunities, though this will require reducing regulatory barriers and administrative red tape.

Supply chains still strained and a logistical challenge

Supply chain stress index (delayed arrival, waiting and delayed handling at ports) in mn. container TEU

Supply chain partners brace for another year of volatility: what to watch out for?

New regulations and protectionist policies are increasingly pushing companies to restructure their supply chains. This rearrangement continues in 2026, while shippers mention increasing buffers alongside diversification of sourcing as key measures to manage risks.

Resilience is the keyword in trade and supply chains nowadays. In container shipping, arrival reliability hovered just above 60% in the final months of 2025, but we are not likely to see historical figures of 75-80% anytime soon.

Supply chains remain under strain. After the pandemic shocks, (looming) shortages of components and raw materials, sanction shifts and sea blockades, trade tensions are keeping supply chains under strain. And this won’t change in the current reality of limited predictability. There’s a reasonable chance that traffic through the Red Sea will be resumed more broadly, which would likely trigger waves of port congestion and push freight rates lower. However, tensions in the Middle East are far from resolved, making this scenario uncertain.

Similarly, the outlook for the war in Ukraine remains unpredictable. Should the conflict ease, freight demand could gain some traction. More broadly, the upcoming renegotiation of the US trade deal with China, as well as a sense of temporality of other deals, represents a major source of uncertainty. In 2026, delivery on previous trade commitments could spark new discussions. The increasing politicisation of trade means volatility will persist. Supply chain management has always been largely driven by cost (and cash flow) efficiency. But reliability in production and delivery to clients is now valued more than before.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more
